title: SEGMENT 1 — The fact that broke developmental biology

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — The fact that broke developmental biology
Alright, let's get started.
Good morning, everyone.

For the next two hours we're going to talk about one of the most consequential experiments in modern biology — an experiment that, in a single 2006 paper, overturned a century of consensus about how cells work and opened up a branch of medicine we are still, twenty years later, only beginning to explore.
The topic is induced pluripotent stem cells, or iPSCs, and the central fact is this.

In 2006, a Japanese researcher named Shinya Yamanaka, working at Kyoto University, took an ordinary adult skin cell — a fibroblast, the kind of cell sitting in your dermis right now holding your skin together — and he forced that cell to express just four genes.
Four.
Not four hundred, not forty.
Four.
The genes were called Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, and c-Myc.
Within a couple of weeks, that skin cell stopped behaving like a skin cell.
It changed shape.
It changed which genes it was reading.
It started dividing faster.
And when Yamanaka looked at it under the microscope and ran the molecular tests, the cell was indistinguishable from an embryonic stem cell.
It could become any tissue in the body.
It had, in effect, traveled backwards in time, from a fully committed adult cell to the pluripotent state that exists for a few days in an early embryo and then never, ever, comes back.
Except now, apparently, it did come back.
You just had to ask in the right way.
That one fact rewrote the rules of developmental biology.
For more than a century, the consensus was that differentiation — the process by which a fertilized egg becomes a liver cell, or a neuron, or a skin cell — was a one-way street.
Cells went down the developmental tree, branch by branch, becoming more specialized at each step, and once they reached the end of a branch they were stuck there forever.
A skin cell could only ever make more skin cells.
A neuron, even worse, couldn't really divide at all.
The developmental tape only played in one direction.
Yamanaka showed that you could rewind it.
With four ingredients, in a dish, in your lab.
That's it.
That's the discovery.
So here's the analogy I want you to carry through this lecture.
Think of development like recording onto a cassette tape.
The fertilized egg is a blank tape — it can become anything.
As the embryo develops, the tape gets recorded on, segment by segment.
By the time you're an adult, every cell in your body has a fully recorded tape that says "you are a skin cell" or "you are a heart muscle cell" or "you are a neuron in the visual cortex.
" Once recorded, the tape was thought to be locked.
The cell could play its recording — do its job — but it couldn't be rerecorded.
Yamanaka found the rewind button.
You can take an adult skin cell, rewind the developmental tape, and end up with a stem cell that could become anything.
And not metaphorically.
Literally.
In a dish.
In about three weeks.

That's why this discovery won the Nobel Prize in 2012, shared with a British biologist named John Gurdon who had, fifty years earlier, done the foundational experiment that suggested rewinding was at least biologically possible.
We'll come to Gurdon in a few minutes.
But first I want you to have the destination in mind.
Why does this matter beyond the textbook?
Three reasons.
First, ethics.
Before iPSCs, if you wanted pluripotent stem cells — cells that could become any tissue in the body, the kind of cells you'd want for regenerative medicine — your only source was a human embryo.
You had to destroy a 5-day-old blastocyst to extract its inner cell mass.
That created an unresolvable political and ethical battle that paralyzed stem cell research in the United States for most of the 2000s.
After Yamanaka, you could derive pluripotent cells from anyone's skin biopsy.
Same biology.
No embryo.
The ethical landscape changed overnight.
Second, personalization.
Embryonic stem cells, even if you can get them, come from somebody else.

If you transplant a tissue made from those cells into a patient, the patient's immune system will recognize the foreign tissue and reject it, exactly as it would reject a kidney transplant from a stranger.
iPSCs, in principle, can be made from the patient's own skin.
The resulting tissues are genetically the patient's own.
No rejection.
No lifelong immunosuppression.
The dream of replacement parts grown from your own cells, on demand, in a dish.
Third, disease in a dish.
You can now take a skin cell from a patient with Parkinson's disease, reprogram it to iPSCs, and then differentiate those iPSCs into the very dopamine neurons that are dying in that patient's brain.
You have, sitting on your bench, a population of that patient's neurons, with their exact genetic background, available to study and to test drugs on.
We could not do that before iPSCs.
Patient brains are off-limits while patients are alive.
iPSCs put the patient's brain cells in your dish.

If you walk out of here in two hours and you can do three things — one, explain to a curious friend what an iPSC is and what Yamanaka actually did, using the rewind-the-tape analogy; two, sketch why the four factors OSKM are the four factors, and what they do once they're inside a skin cell; and three, look at a news headline about a new iPSC-based therapy — say a clinical trial for diabetes, or for macular degeneration, or for spinal cord injury — and have a real intuition for what's happening
underneath — then we've succeeded.
Everything else is detail hung onto that scaffold.
Let's start by going back to the conventional wisdom that Yamanaka broke, because you can't appreciate how shocking his result was without knowing what people believed before.
